description: Thin tall plate building in an atmospheric boundary layer. Pedestrian wind validation case.
source_experiment: AIJ Case B 4:4:1; primary experiment AIJ Journal of Technology and Design No.18, 169-174 (in Japanese)

geometry.note_orientation: VERIFY from AIJ schematic which plan face is across-wind vs along-wind before meshing; this changes the separation physics.

reference_velocity.primary_definition: inflow U at building height H=4b=200mm; standard AIJ pedestrian normalization
reference_velocity.pedestrian_level_definition: inflow U interpolated at z=12.5mm (pedestrian height)
reference_velocity.note: ratio_UH columns in points use U_H=5.133. CONFIRM AIJ Case B convention before computing final metrics (Case A had a 2.935 vs 2.75 mix-up).

pedestrian_section.columns: no, x/y/z in mm and /b, U V W [m/s], Uh=sqrt(U^2+V^2), k [m2/s2], ratio_UH=Uh/5.133

open_questions_before_metrics: Confirm reference velocity: U_H=5.133 (building height) vs pedestrian-level, from AIJ Case B spec.
Confirm building plan orientation (which face is across-wind) from the AIJ schematic.
Confirm pedestrian normalization method: direct /U_H vs incident-local (Case A required incident-local because the ABL drifts along the fetch).
Confirm domain extent from AIJ Case B spec (measurement points span x=-75..550mm, y=-350..0mm); build the tunnel domain from the case spec, not Case A's.
method_reminder: Use the baked-in convergence protocol: equilibrium seed (generator-enforced), monitor probe, take q ONLY after the probe freezes (stationarity). If a limit cycle -> window-average + uncertainty band, never a single snapshot. Expect strong unsteady shedding (thin plate); k-epsilon may converge via over-damping, k-omega likely will not (as in Case A).
